caldwell company publishers to my dear husband george henry lewes in this nineteenth year of our blessed union
prelude who that care much to know the history of man and how the mysterious mixture behave under the vary experiment of time have not dwelt at least briefly on the life of saint theresa have not smile with some gentleness at the thought of the little girl walk forth one morning hand-in-hand with her still small brother to go and seek martyrdom in the country of the moors
out they toddle from rugged avila wide-eyed and helpless-look as two fawn but with human heart already beat to a national idea until domestic reality meet they in the shape of uncle and turn they back from their great resolve
that child-pilgrimage be a fit beginning
theresa s passionate ideal nature demand an epic life what be many-volume romance of chivalry and the social conquest of a brilliant girl to she
her flame quickly burn up that light fuel and feed from within soar after some illimitable satisfaction some object which would never justify weariness which would reconcile self-despair with the rapturous consciousness of life beyond self
she find her epos in the reform of a religious order
that spanish woman who live three hundred year ago be certainly not the last of her kind
many theresas have be bear who find for themselves no epic life wherein there be a constant unfolding of far-resonant action perhaps only a life of mistake the offspring of a certain spiritual grandeur ill-match with the meanness of opportunity perhaps a tragic failure which find no sacred poet and sink unwept into oblivion
with dim light and tangle circumstance they try to shape their thought and deed in noble agreement but after all to common eye their struggle seem mere inconsistency and formlessness for these later-bear theresas be help by no coherent social faith and order which could perform the function of knowledge for the ardently willing soul
their ardor alternate between a vague ideal and the common yearning of womanhood so that the one be disapprove as extravagance and the other condemn as a lapse
some have feel that these blunder life be due to the inconvenient indefiniteness with which the supreme power have fashion the nature of woman if there be one level of feminine incompetence as strict as the ability to count three and no more the social lot of woman might be treat with scientific certitude
meanwhile the indefiniteness remain and the limit of variation be really much wide than any one would imagine from the sameness of woman s coiffure and the favorite love-story in prose and verse
here and there a cygnet be rear uneasily among the duckling in the brown pond and never find the living stream in fellowship with its own oary-footed kind
here and there be bear a saint theresa foundress of nothing whose love heart-beat and sob after an unattained goodness tremble off and be disperse among hindrance instead of centre in some long-recognizable deed

miss brooke have that kind of beauty which seem to be throw into relief by poor dress
her hand and wrist be so finely form that she could wear sleeve not less bare of style than those in which the blessed virgin appear to italian painter and her profile as well as her stature and bearing seem to gain the more dignity from her plain garment which by the side of provincial fashion give she the impressiveness of a fine quotation from the bible or from one of our elder poets in a paragraph of to-day s newspaper
she be usually speak of as be remarkably clever but with the addition that her sister celia have more common-sense
nevertheless celia wear scarcely more trimming and it be only to close observer that her dress differed from her sister s and have a shade of coquetry in its arrangement for miss brooke s plain dressing be due to mixed condition in most of which her sister share
the pride of be lady have something to do with it the brooke connection though not exactly aristocratic be unquestionably good if you inquire backward for a generation or two you would not find any yard-measuring or parcel-tie forefather--anything low than an admiral or a clergyman and there be even an ancestor discernible as a puritan gentleman who serve under cromwell but afterwards conform and manage to come out of all political trouble as the proprietor of a respectable family estate
young woman of such birth live in a quiet country-house and attend a village church hardly large than a parlor naturally regard frippery as the ambition of a huckster s daughter
then there be well-breed economy which in those day make show in dress the first item to be deduct from when any margin be require for expense more distinctive of rank
such reason would have be enough to account for plain dress quite apart from religious feeling but in miss brooke s case religion alone would have determine it and celia mildly acquiesce in all her sister s sentiment only infuse they with that common-sense which be able to accept momentous doctrine without any eccentric agitation
dorothea know many passage of pascal s pensees and of jeremy taylor by heart and to she the destiny of mankind see by the light of christianity make the solicitude of feminine fashion appear an occupation for bedlam
she could not reconcile the anxiety of a spiritual life involve eternal consequence with a keen interest in gimp and artificial protrusion of drapery
her mind be theoretic and yearn by its nature after some lofty conception of the world which might frankly include the parish of tipton and her own rule of conduct there she be enamour of intensity and greatness and rash in embrace whatever seem to she to have those aspect likely to seek martyrdom to make retractation and then to incur martyrdom after all in a quarter where she have not seek it
certainly such element in the character of a marriageable girl tend to interfere with her lot and hinder it from be decide accord to custom by good look vanity and merely canine affection
with all this she the elder of the sister be not yet twenty and they have both be educate since they be about twelve year old and have lose their parent on plan at once narrow and promiscuous first in an english family and afterwards in a swiss family at lausanne their bachelor uncle and guardian try in this way to remedy the disadvantage of their orphan condition
it be hardly a year since they have come to live at tipton grange with their uncle a man nearly sixty of acquiescent temper miscellaneous opinion and uncertain vote
he have travel in his young year and be hold in this part of the county to have contract a too ramble habit of mind

brooke s conclusion be as difficult to predict as the weather it be only safe to say that he would act with benevolent intention and that he would spend as little money as possible in carry they out

and how should dorothea not marry a girl so handsome and with such prospect
nothing could hinder it but her love of extreme and her insistence on regulate life accord to notion which might cause a wary man to hesitate before he make she an offer or even might lead she at last to refuse all offer
a young lady of some birth and fortune who kneel suddenly down on a brick floor by the side of a sick laborer and pray fervidly as if she think herself live in the time of the apostle--who have strange whim of fast like a papist and of sit up at night to read old theological book
such a wife might awaken you some fine morning with a new scheme for the application of her income which would interfere with political economy and the keeping of saddle-horse a man would naturally think twice before he risk himself in such fellowship
woman be expect to have weak opinion but the great safeguard of society and of domestic life be that opinion be not act on

my dear miss brooke i have your guardian s permission to address you on a subject than which i have none more at heart
i be not i trust mistaken in the recognition of some deep correspondence than that of date in the fact that a consciousness of need in my own life have arise contemporaneously with the possibility of my become acquaint with you
for in the first hour of meet you i have an impression of your eminent and perhaps exclusive fitness to supply that need connect i may say with such activity of the affection as even the preoccupation of a work too special to be abdicate could not uninterruptedly dissimulate and each succeed opportunity for observation have give the impression an add depth by convince i more emphatically of that fitness which i have preconceive and thus evoke more decisively those affection to which i have but now refer
our conversation have i think make sufficiently clear to you the tenor of my life and purpose a tenor unsuited i be aware to the common order of mind
but i have discern in you an elevation of thought and a capability of devotedness which i have hitherto not conceive to be compatible either with the early bloom of youth or with those grace of sex that may be say at once to win and to confer distinction when combine as they notably be in you with the mental quality above indicate
it be i confess beyond my hope to meet with this rare combination of element both solid and attractive adapt to supply aid in graver labor and to cast a charm over vacant hour and but for the event of my introduction to you which let i again say i trust not to be superficially coincident with foreshadowing need but providentially relate thereto as stage towards the completion of a life s plan i should presumably have go on to the last without any attempt to lighten my solitariness by a matrimonial union
such my dear miss brooke be the accurate statement of my feeling and i rely on your kind indulgence in venture now to ask you how far your own be of a nature to confirm my happy presentiment
to be accept by you as your husband and the earthly guardian of your welfare i should regard as the high of providential gift
in return i can at least offer you an affection hitherto unwasted and the faithful consecration of a life which however short in the sequel have no backward page whereon if you choose to turn they you will find record such as might justly cause you either bitterness or shame
i await the expression of your sentiment with an anxiety which it would be the part of wisdom be it possible to divert by a more arduous labor than usual
but in this order of experience i be still young and in look forward to an unfavorable possibility i cannot but feel that resignation to solitude will be more difficult after the temporary illumination of hope
in any case i shall remain your with sincere devotion edward casaubon

old provincial society have its share of this subtle movement have not only its striking downfall its brilliant young professional dandy who end by live up an entry with a drab and six child for their establishment but also those less mark vicissitude which be constantly shift the boundary of social intercourse and beget new consciousness of interdependence
some slip a little downward some get high footing people deny aspirate gain wealth and fastidious gentleman stand for borough some be catch in political current some in ecclesiastical and perhaps find themselves surprisingly group in consequence while a few personage or family that stand with rocky firmness amid all this fluctuation be slowly present new aspect in spite of solidity and alter with the double change of self and beholder
municipal town and rural parish gradually make fresh thread of connection--gradually as the old stocking give way to the saving-bank and the worship of the solar guinea become extinct while squire and baronet and even lord who have once live blamelessly afar from the civic mind gather the faultiness of close acquaintanceship
settler too come from distant county some with an alarming novelty of skill other with an offensive advantage in cunning

the ride to stone court which fred and rosamond take the next morning lie through a pretty bit of midland landscape almost all meadow and pasture with hedgerow still allow to grow in bushy beauty and to spread out coral fruit for the bird
little detail give each field a particular physiognomy dear to the eye that have look on they from childhood the pool in the corner where the grass be dank and tree lean whisperingly the great oak shadow a bare place in mid-pasture the high bank where the ash-tree grow the sudden slope of the old marl-pit make a red background for the burdock the huddle roof and rick of the homestead without a traceable way of approach the gray gate and fence against the depth of the border wood and the stray hovel its old old thatch full of mossy hill and valley with wondrous modulation of light and shadow such as we travel far to see in later life and see large but not more beautiful
these be the thing that make the gamut of joy in landscape to midland-breed soul--the thing they toddle among or perhaps learn by heart stand between their father s knee while he drive leisurely

there be a general impression however that lydgate be not altogether a common country doctor and in middlemarch at that time such an impression be significant of great thing be expect from he
for everybody s family doctor be remarkably clever and be understand to have immeasurable skill in the management and training of the most skittish or vicious disease
the evidence of his cleverness be of the high intuitive order lie in his lady-patient immovable conviction and be unassailable by any objection except that their intuition be oppose by other equally strong each lady who see medical truth in wrench and the strengthen treatment regard toller and the lower system as medical perdition
for the heroic time of copious bleeding and blistering have not yet depart still less the time of thorough-go theory when disease in general be call by some bad name and treat accordingly without shilly-shally--as if for example it be to be call insurrection which must not be fire on with blank-cartridge but have its blood draw at once
the strengthener and the lowerer be all clever man in somebody s opinion which be really as much as can be say for any live talent

lydgate could know as much as dr

still i repeat there be a general impression that lydgate be something rather more uncommon than any general practitioner in middlemarch
and this be true
he be but seven-and-twenty an age at which many man be not quite common--at which they be hopeful of achievement resolute in avoidance think that mammon shall never put a bit in their mouth and get astride their back but rather that mammon if they have anything to do with he shall draw their chariot

his father a military man have make but little provision for three child and when the boy tertius ask to have a medical education it seem easy to his guardian to grant his request by apprentice he to a country practitioner than to make any objection on the score of family dignity
he be one of the rare lad who early get a decide bent and make up their mind that there be something particular in life which they would like to do for its own sake and not because their father do it
most of we who turn to any subject with love remember some morning or evening hour when we get on a high stool to reach down an untried volume or sit with part lip listen to a new talker or for very lack of book begin to listen to the voice within as the first traceable beginning of our love
something of that sort happen to lydgate
he be a quick fellow and when hot from play would toss himself in a corner and in five minute be deep in any sort of book that he could lay his hand on if it be rasselas or gulliver so much the well but bailey s dictionary would do or the bible with the apocrypha in it
something he must read when he be not rid the pony or run and hunting or listen to the talk of man
all this be true of he at ten year of age he have then read through chrysal or the adventures of a guinea which be neither milk for babe nor any chalky mixture mean to pass for milk and it have already occur to he that book be stuff and that life be stupid
his school study have not much modify that opinion for though he do his classic and mathematic he be not pre-eminent in they
it be say of he that lydgate could do anything he like but he have certainly not yet like to do anything remarkable
he be a vigorous animal with a ready understanding but no spark have yet kindle in he an intellectual passion knowledge seem to he a very superficial affair easily master judge from the conversation of his elder he have apparently get already more than be necessary for mature life
probably this be not an exceptional result of expensive teaching at that period of short-waiste coat and other fashion which have not yet recur
but one vacation a wet day send he to the small home library to hunt once more for a book which might have some freshness for he in vain
unless indeed he take down a dusty row of volume with gray-paper back and dingy label--the volume of an old cyclopaedia which he have never disturb
it would at least be a novelty to disturb they
they be on the high shelf and he stand on a chair to get they down
but he open the volume which he first take from the shelf somehow one be apt to read in a makeshift attitude just where it might seem inconvenient to do so
the page he open on be under the head of anatomy and the first passage that draw his eye be on the valve of the heart
he be not much acquaint with valve of any sort but he know that valvae be fold-door and through this crevice come a sudden light startle he with his first vivid notion of finely adjusted mechanism in the human frame
a liberal education have of course leave he free to read the indecent passage in the school classic but beyond a general sense of secrecy and obscenity in connection with his internal structure have leave his imagination quite unbiassed so that for anything he know his brain lie in small bag at his temple and he have no more thought of represent to himself how his blood circulate than how paper serve instead of gold
but the moment of vocation have come and before he get down from his chair the world be make new to he by a presentiment of endless process fill the vast space plank out of his sight by that wordy ignorance which he have suppose to be knowledge
from that hour lydgate feel the growth of an intellectual passion
we be not afraid of tell over and over again how a man come to fall in love with a woman and be wed to she or else be fatally part from she
be it due to excess of poetry or of stupidity that we be never weary of describe what king james call a woman s makdom and her fairnesse never weary of listen to the twanging of the old troubadour string and be comparatively uninterested in that other kind of makdom and fairnesse which must be woo with industrious thought and patient renunciation of small desire
in the story of this passion too the development vary sometimes it be the glorious marriage sometimes frustration and final parting
and not seldom the catastrophe be bind up with the other passion sing by the troubadour
for in the multitude of middle-aged man who go about their vocation in a daily course determine for they much in the same way as the tie of their cravat there be always a good number who once mean to shape their own deed and alter the world a little
the story of their come to be shapen after the average and fit to be pack by the gross be hardly ever tell even in their consciousness for perhaps their ardor in generous unpaid toil cool as imperceptibly as the ardor of other youthful love till one day their early self walk like a ghost in its old home and make the new furniture ghastly
nothing in the world more subtle than the process of their gradual change
in the beginning they inhale it unknowingly you and i may have send some of our breath towards infect they when we utter our conform falsity or draw our silly conclusion or perhaps it come with the vibration from a woman s glance
lydgate do not mean to be one of those failure and there be the well hope of he because his scientific interest soon take the form of a professional enthusiasm he have a youthful belief in his bread-win work not to be stifle by that initiation in makeshift call his prentice day and he carry to his study in london edinburgh and paris the conviction that the medical profession as it might be be the fine in the world present the most perfect interchange between science and art offer the most direct alliance between intellectual conquest and the social good
lydgate s nature demand this combination he be an emotional creature with a flesh-and-blood sense of fellowship which withstand all the abstraction of special study
he care not only for case but for john and elizabeth especially elizabeth
there be another attraction in his profession it want reform and give a man an opportunity for some indignant resolve to reject its venal decoration and other humbug and to be the possessor of genuine though undemanded qualification
he go to study in paris with the determination that when he provincial home again he would settle in some provincial town as a general practitioner and resist the irrational severance between medical and surgical knowledge in the interest of his own scientific pursuit as well as of the general advance he would keep away from the range of london intrigue jealousy and social truckling and win celebrity however slowly as jenner have do by the independent value of his work
for it must be remember that this be a dark period and in spite of venerable college which use great effort to secure purity of knowledge by make it scarce and to exclude error by a rigid exclusiveness in relation to fee and appointment it happen that very ignorant young gentleman be promote in town and many more get a legal right to practise over large area in the country
also the high standard hold up to the public mind by the college of which which give its peculiar sanction to the expensive and highly rarefied medical instruction obtain by graduate of oxford and cambridge do not hinder quackery from have an excellent time of it for since professional practice chiefly consist in give a great many drug the public infer that it might be well off with more drug still if they could only be get cheaply and hence swallow large cubic measure of physic prescribe by unscrupulous ignorance which have take no degree
consider that statistic have not yet embrace a calculation as to the number of ignorant or canting doctor which absolutely must exist in the tooth of all change it seem to lydgate that a change in the unit be the most direct mode of change the number
he mean to be a unit who would make a certain amount of difference towards that spread change which would one day tell appreciably upon the average and in the mean time have the pleasure of make an advantageous difference to the viscera of his own patient
but he do not simply aim at a more genuine kind of practice than be common
he be ambitious of a wide effect he be fire with the possibility that he might work out the proof of an anatomical conception and make a link in the chain of discovery
do it seem incongruous to you that a middlemarch surgeon should dream of himself as a discoverer
most of we indeed know little of the great originator until they have be lift up among the constellation and already rule our fate
but that herschel for example who break the barrier of the heavens did he not once play a provincial church-organ and give music-lesson to stumble pianist
each of those shin ones have to walk on the earth among neighbor who perhaps think much more of his gait and his garment than of anything which be to give he a title to everlaste fame each of they have his little local personal history sprinkle with small temptation and sordid care which make the retarding friction of his course towards final companionship with the immortal
lydgate be not blind to the danger of such friction but he have plenty of confidence in his resolution to avoid it as far as possible be seven-and-twenty he feel himself experienced
and he be not go to have his vanity provoke by contact with the showy worldly success of the capital but to live among people who could hold no rivalry with that pursuit of a great idea which be to be a twin object with the assiduous practice of his profession
there be fascination in the hope that the two purpose would illuminate each other the careful observation and inference which be his daily work the use of the lens to further his judgment in special case would further his thought as an instrument of large inquiry
be not this the typical pre-eminence of his profession
he would be a good middlemarch doctor and by that very mean keep himself in the track of far-reach investigation
on one point he may fairly claim approval at this particular stage of his career he do not mean to imitate those philanthropic model who make a profit out of poisonous pickle to support themselves while they be expose adulteration or hold share in a gambling-hell that they may have leisure to represent the cause of public morality
he intend to begin in his own case some particular reform which be quite certainly within his reach and much less of a problem than the demonstrating of an anatomical conception
one of these reform be to act stoutly on the strength of a recent legal decision and simply prescribe without dispense drug or take percentage from druggist
this be an innovation for one who have choose to adopt the style of general practitioner in a country town and would be feel as offensive criticism by his professional brother
but lydgate mean to innovate in his treatment also and he be wise enough to see that the good security for his practising honestly accord to his belief be to get rid of systematic temptation to the contrary
perhaps that be a more cheerful time for observer and theorizer than the present we be apt to think it the fine era of the world when america be begin to be discover when a bold sailor even if he be wreck might alight on a new kingdom and about 1829 the dark territory of pathology be a fine america for a spirited young adventurer
lydgate be ambitious above all to contribute towards enlarge the scientific rational basis of his profession
the more he become interested in special question of disease such as the nature of fever or fever the more keenly he feel the need for that fundamental knowledge of structure which just at the beginning of the century have be illuminate by the brief and glorious career of bichat who die when he be only one-and-thirty but like another alexander leave a realm large enough for many heir
that great frenchman first carry out the conception that live body fundamentally consider be not association of organ which can be understand by study they first apart and then as it be federally but must be regard as consist of certain primary webs or tissue out of which the various organ--brain heart lung and so on--be compact as the various accommodation of a house be build up in various proportion of wood iron stone brick zinc and the rest each material have its peculiar composition and proportion
no man one see can understand and estimate the entire structure or its part--what be its frailty and what its repair without know the nature of the material
and the conception work out by bichat with his detailed study of the different tissue act necessarily on medical question as the turning of gas-light would act on a dim oil-light street show new connection and hitherto hide fact of structure which must be take into account in consider the symptom of malady and the action of medicament
but result which depend on human conscience and intelligence work slowly and now at the end of 1829 most medical practice be still strut or shamble along the old path and there be still scientific work to be do which might have seem to be a direct sequence of bichat s
this great seer do not go beyond the consideration of the tissue as ultimate fact in the living organism mark the limit of anatomical analysis but it be open to another mind to say have not these structure some common basis from which they have all start as your sarsnet gauze net satin and velvet from the raw cocoon
here would be another light as of oxy-hydrogen show the very grain of thing and revise all former explanation
of this sequence to bichat s work already vibrate along many current of the european mind lydgate be enamour he long to demonstrate the more intimate relation of living structure and help to define man s think more accurately after the true order
the work have not yet be do but only prepare for those who know how to use the preparation
what be the primitive tissue
in that way lydgate put the question--not quite in the way require by the await answer but such missing of the right word befall many seeker
and he count on quiet interval to be watchfully seize for take up the thread of investigation--on many hint to be win from diligent application not only of the scalpel but of the microscope which research have begin to use again with new enthusiasm of reliance
such be lydgate s plan of his future to do good small work for middlemarch and great work for the world
he be certainly a happy fellow at this time to be seven-and-twenty without any fix vice with a generous resolution that his action should be beneficent and with idea in his brain that make life interesting quite apart from the cultus of horseflesh and other mystic rite of costly observance which the eight hundred pound leave he after buy his practice would certainly not have go far in pay for
he be at a starting-point which make many a man s career a fine subject for bet if there be any gentleman give to that amusement who could appreciate the complicated probability of an arduous purpose with all the possible thwarting and furthering of circumstance all the nicety of inward balance by which a man swim and make his point or else be carry headlong
the risk would remain even with close knowledge of lydgate s character for character too be a process and an unfolding
the man be still in the making as much as the middlemarch doctor and immortal discoverer and there be both virtue and fault capable of shrink or expand
the fault will not i hope be a reason for the withdrawal of your interest in he

where then lay the spot of commonness
say a young lady enamour of that careless grace
how could there be any commonness in a man so well-breed so ambitious of social distinction so generous and unusual in his view of social duty
as easily as there may be stupidity in a man of genius if you take he unawares on the wrong subject or as many a man who have the good will to advance the social millennium might be ill-inspire in imagine its light pleasure unable to go beyond offenbach s music or the brilliant pun in the last burlesque
lydgate s spot of commonness lie in the complexion of his prejudice which in spite of noble intention and sympathy be half of they such as be find in ordinary man of the world that distinction of mind which belong to his intellectual ardor do not penetrate his feeling and judgment about furniture or woman or the desirability of its be know without his telling that he be well bear than other country surgeon

for those who want to be acquaint with lydgate it will be good to know what be that case of impetuous folly for it may stand as an example of the fitful swerving of passion to which he be prone together with the chivalrous kindness which help to make he morally lovable
the story can be tell without many word

as he throw down his book stretch his leg towards the ember in the grate and clasp his hand at the back of his head in that agreeable afterglow of excitement when think lapse from examination of a specific object into a suffusive sense of its connection with all the rest of our existence--seem as it be to throw itself on its back after vigorous swimming and float with the repose of unexhausted strength--lydgate feel a triumphant delight in his study and something like pity for those less lucky man who be not of his profession

caleb garth often shake his head in meditation on the value the indispensable might of that myriad-head myriad-hand labor by which the social body be feed clothe and house
it have lay hold of his imagination in boyhood
the echo of the great hammer where roof or keel be a-making the signal-shout of the workman the roar of the furnace the thunder and plash of the engine be a sublime music to he the felling and lading of timber and the huge trunk vibrate star-like in the distance along the highway the crane at work on the wharf the pile-up produce in warehouse the precision and variety of muscular effort wherever exact work have to be turn out all these sight of his youth have act on he as poetry without the aid of the poet have make a philosophy for he without the aid of philosopher a religion without the aid of theology
his early ambition have be to have as effective a share as possible in this sublime labor which be peculiarly dignified by he with the name of business and though he have only be a short time under a surveyor and have be chiefly his own teacher he know more of land building and mining than most of the special man in the county
his classification of human employment be rather crude and like the category of more celebrated man would not be acceptable in these advanced time
he divide they into business politic preach learning and amusement he have nothing to say against the last four but he regard they as a reverential pagan regard other god than his own
in the same way he think very well of all rank but he would not himself have like to be of any rank in which he have not such close contact with business as to get often honorably decorate with mark of dust and mortar the damp of the engine or the sweet soil of the wood and field
though he have never regard himself as other than an orthodox christian and would argue on prevenient grace if the subject be propose to he i think his virtual divinity be good practical scheme accurate work and the faithful completion of undertaking his prince of darkness be a slack workman
but there be no spirit of denial in caleb and the world seem so wondrous to he that he be ready to accept any number of system like any number of firmament if they do not obviously interfere with the good land-drainage solid building correct measuring and judicious boring for coal
in fact he have a reverential soul with a strong practical intelligence
but he could not manage finance he know value well but he have no keenness of imagination for monetary result in the shape of profit and loss and have ascertain this to his cost he determine to give up all form of his beloved business which require that talent
he give himself up entirely to the many kind of work which he could do without handle capital and be one of those precious man within his own district whom everybody would choose to work for they because he do his work well charge very little and often decline to charge at all
it be no wonder then that the garths be poor and live in a small way however they do not mind it

that night after twelve o clock mary garth relieve the watch in mr
featherstone s room and sit there alone through the small hour
she often choose this task in which she find some pleasure notwithstanding the old man s testiness whenever he demand her attention
there be interval in which she could sit perfectly still enjoy the outer stillness and the subdue light
the red fire with its gently audible movement seem like a solemn existence calmly independent of the petty passion the imbecile desire the straining after worthless uncertainty which be daily move her contempt
mary be fond of her own thought and could amuse herself well sit in twilight with her hand in her lap for have early have strong reason to believe that thing be not likely to be arrange for her peculiar satisfaction she waste no time in astonishment and annoyance at that fact
and she have already come to take life very much as a comedy in which she have a proud nay a generous resolution not to act the mean or treacherous part
mary might have become cynical if she have not have parent whom she honor and a well of affectionate gratitude within she which be all the fuller because she have learn to make no unreasonable claim

and here i be naturally lead to reflect on the mean of elevate a low subject
historical parallel be remarkably efficient in this way
the chief objection to they be that the diligent narrator may lack space or what be often the same thing may not be able to think of they with any degree of particularity though he may have a philosophical confidence that if know they would be illustrative
it seem an easy and short way to dignity to observe that--since there never be a true story which could not be tell in parable where you might put a monkey for a margrave and vice versa--whatever have be or be to be narrate by i about low people may be ennoble by be consider a parable so that if any bad habit and ugly consequence be bring into view the reader may have the relief of regard they as not more than figuratively ungenteel and may feel himself virtually in company with person of some style
thus while i tell the truth about loobie my reader s imagination need not be entirely exclude from an occupation with lord and the petty sum which any bankrupt of high standing would be sorry to retire upon may be lift to the level of high commercial transaction by the inexpensive addition of proportional cipher
as to any provincial history in which the agent be all of high moral rank that must be of a date long posterior to the first reform bill and peter featherstone you perceive be dead and bury some month before lord grey come into office

young love-making--that gossamer web

the web itself be make of spontaneous belief and indefinable joy yearning of one life towards another vision of completeness indefinite trust
and lydgate fall to spin that web from his inward self with wonderful rapidity in spite of experience suppose to be finish off with the drama of laure--in spite too of medicine and biology for the inspection of macerate muscle or of eye present in a dish like santa lucia s and other incident of scientific inquiry be observe to be less incompatible with poetic love than a native dulness or a lively addiction to the low prose

in warm himself at french social theory he have bring away no smell of scorching
we may handle even extreme opinion with impunity while our furniture our dinner-giving and preference for armorial bearing in our own case link we indissolubly with the establish order
and lydgate s tendency be not towards extreme opinion he would have like no barefooted doctrine be particular about his boot he be no radical in relation to anything but medical reform and the prosecution of discovery
in the rest of practical life he walk by hereditary habit half from that personal pride and unreflecte egoism which i have already call commonness and half from that naivete which belong to preoccupation with favorite idea

in the hundred to which middlemarch belong railway be as exciting a topic as the reform bill or the imminent horror of cholera and those who hold the most decide view on the subject be woman and landholder
woman both old and young regard travel by steam as presumptuous and dangerous and argue against it by say that nothing should induce they to get into a railway carriage while proprietor differ from each other in their argument as much as mr
solomon featherstone differ from lord medlicote be yet unanimous in the opinion that in sell land whether to the enemy of mankind or to a company oblige to purchase these pernicious agency must be make to pay a very high price to landowner for permission to injure mankind

even the rumor of reform have not yet excite any millennial expectation in frick there be no definite promise in it as of gratuitous grain to fatten hiram ford s pig or of a publican at the weights and scales who would brew beer for nothing or of an offer on the part of the three neighbor farmer to raise wage during winter

the man of frick be not ill-fed and be less give to fanaticism than to a strong muscular suspicion less inclined to believe that they be peculiarly care for by heaven than to regard heaven itself as rather disposed to take they in--a disposition observable in the weather

it be too good to believe ah but mind you say caleb turn his head warningly i must take it on my shoulder and be responsible and see after everything and that will grieve your mother a bit though she maynot say so
fred have need be careful perhaps it be too much father say mary check in her joy
there would be no happiness in bring you any fresh trouble nay nay work be my delight child when it donot vex your mother
and then if you and fred get marry here caleb s voice shake just perceptibly hewill be steady and saving and youhave get your mother s cleverness and mine too in a woman s sort of way and youwill keep he in order
hewill be come by-and-by so i want to tell you first because i think youwould like to tell he by yourself
after that i could talk it well over with he and we could go into business and the nature of thing oh you dear good father cry mary put her hand round her father s neck while he bend his head placidly willing to be caress
i wonder if any other girl think her father the good man in the world nonsense child youwill think your husband well impossible say mary relapse into her usual tone husband be an inferior class of man who require keep in order when they be enter the house with letty who have run to join they mary see fred at the orchard-gate and go to meet he
what fine clothe you wear you extravagant youth say mary as fred stand still and raise his hat to she with playful formality
you be not learn economy now that be too bad mary say fred
just look at the edge of these coat-cuff
it be only by dint of good brushing that i look respectable
i be save up three suit--one for a wedding-suit how very droll you will look like a gentleman in an old fashion-book oh no they will keep two year two year
be reasonable fred say mary turn to walk
donot encourage flattering expectation why not
one live on they well than on unflattering one
if we cannot be marry in two year the truth will be quite bad enough when it come i have hear a story of a young gentleman who once encourage flattering expectation and they do he harm mary if youhave get something discouraging to tell i i shall bolt i shall go into the house to mr
garth
i be out of spirit
my father be so cut up--home be not like itself
i cannot bear any more bad news should you call it bad news to be tell that you be to live at stone court and manage the farm and be remarkably prudent and save money every year till all the stock and furniture be your own and you be a distinguished agricultural character as mr
borthrop trumbull say--rather stout i fear and with the greek and latin sadly weather-worn you donot mean anything except nonsense mary say fred color slightly nevertheless
that be what my father have just tell i of as what may happen and he never talk nonsense say mary look up at fred now while he grasp her hand as they walk till it rather hurt she but she would not complain
oh i could be a tremendously good fellow then mary and we could be marry directly not so fast sir how do you know that i would not rather defer our marriage for some year
that would leave you time to misbehave and then if i like some one else well i should have an excuse for jilt you pray donot joke mary say fred with strong feeling
tell i seriously that all this be true and that you be happy because of it--because you love i well it be all true fred and i be happy because of it--because i love you well say mary in a tone of obedient recitation
they linger on the door-step under the steep-roofed porch and fred almost in a whisper said-- when we be first engage with the umbrella-ring mary you use to-- the spirit of joy begin to laugh more decidedly in mary s eye but the fatal ben come run to the door with brownie yap behind he and bounce against they said-- fred and mary
be you ever come in or may i eat your cake finale
every limit be a beginning as well as an ending
who can quit young life after be long in company with they and not desire to know what befell they in their after-year
for the fragment of a life however typical be not the sample of an even web promise may not be keep and an ardent outset may be follow by declension latent power may find their long-wait opportunity a past error may urge a grand retrieval
marriage which have be the bourne of so many narrative be still a great beginning as it be to adam and eve who keep their honeymoon in eden but have their first little one among the thorn and thistle of the wilderness
it be still the beginning of the home epic--the gradual conquest or irremediable loss of that complete union which make the advance year a climax and age the harvest of sweet memory in common
some set out like crusaders of old with a glorious equipment of hope and enthusiasm and get break by the way want patience with each other and the world
all who have care for fred vincy and mary garth will like to know that these two make no such failure but achieve a solid mutual happiness
fred surprise his neighbor in various way
he become rather distinguish in his side of the county as a theoretic and practical farmer and produce a work on the cultivation of green crops and the economy of cattle-feeding which win he high congratulation at agricultural meeting
in middlemarch admiration be more reserved most person there be inclined to believe that the merit of fred s authorship be due to his wife since they have never expect fred vincy to write on turnip and mangel-wurzel
but when mary write a little book for her boy call story of great men take from plutarch and have it print and publish by gripp co middlemarch every one in the town be willing to give the credit of this work to fred observe that he have be to the university where the ancient be study and might have be a clergyman if he have choose
in this way it be make clear that middlemarch have never be deceive and that there be no need to praise anybody for write a book since it be always do by somebody else
moreover fred remain unswervingly steady
some year after his marriage he tell mary that his happiness be half owe to farebrother who give he a strong pull-up at the right moment
i cannot say that he be never again mislead by his hopefulness the yield of crop or the profit of a cattle sale usually fall below his estimate and he be always prone to believe that he could make money by the purchase of a horse which turn out badly--though this mary observe be of course the fault of the horse not of fred s judgment
he keep his love of horsemanship but he rarely allow himself a day s hunting and when he do so it be remarkable that he submit to be laugh at for cowardliness at the fence seem to see mary and the boy sit on the five-bar gate or show their curly head between hedge and ditch
there be three boy mary be not discontent that she bring forth man-child only and when fred wish to have a girl like she she say laughingly that would be too great a trial to your mother mrs
vincy in her decline year and in the diminished lustre of her housekeeping be much comfort by her perception that two at least of fred s boy be real vincys and do not feature the garths but mary secretly rejoice that the young of the three be very much what her father must have be when he wear a round jacket and show a marvellous nicety of aim in play at marble or in throw stone to bring down the mellow pear
ben and letty garth who be uncle and aunt before they be well in their teen dispute much as to whether nephew or niece be more desirable ben contend that it be clear girl be good for less than boy else they would not be always in petticoat which show how little they be mean for whereupon letty who argue much from book get angry in reply that god make coat of skin for both adam and eve alike--also it occur to she that in the east the man too wear petticoat
but this latter argument obscure the majesty of the former be one too many for ben answer contemptuously the more spooney they and immediately appeal to his mother whether boy be not well than girl
mrs
garth pronounce that both be alike naughty but that boy be undoubtedly strong could run fast and throw with more precision to a great distance
with this oracular sentence ben be well satisfied not mind the naughtiness but letty take it ill her feeling of superiority be strong than her muscle
fred never become rich--his hopefulness have not lead he to expect that but he gradually save enough to become owner of the stock and furniture at stone court and the work which mr
garth put into his hand carry he in plenty through those bad time which be always present with farmer
mary in her matronly day become as solid in figure as her mother but unlike she give the boy little formal teaching so that mrs
garth be alarmed lest they should never be well ground in grammar and geography
nevertheless they be find quite forward enough when they go to school perhaps because they have like nothing so well as be with their mother
when fred be rid home on winter evening he have a pleasant vision beforehand of the bright hearth in the wainscoted parlor and be sorry for other man who could not have mary for their wife especially for mr
farebrother
he be ten time worthy of you than i be fred could now say to she magnanimously
to be sure he be mary answer and for that reason he could do well without i
but you--i shudder to think what you would have be--a curate in debt for horse-hire and cambric pocket-handkerchief on inquiry it might possibly be find that fred and mary still inhabit stone court--that the creep plant still cast the foam of their blossom over the fine stone-wall into the field where the walnut-trees stand in stately row--and that on sunny day the two lover who be first engage with the umbrella-ring may be see in white-haired placidity at the open window from which mary garth in the day of old peter featherstone have often be order to look out for mr
lydgate
lydgate s hair never become white
he die when he be only fifty leave his wife and child provide for by a heavy insurance on his life
he have gain an excellent practice alternate accord to the season between london and a continental bathing-place have write a treatise on gout a disease which have a good deal of wealth on its side
his skill be rely on by many pay patient but he always regard himself as a failure he have not do what he once mean to do
his acquaintance think he enviable to have so charm a wife and nothing happen to shake their opinion
rosamond never commit a second compromise indiscretion
she simply continue to be mild in her temper inflexible in her judgment dispose to admonish her husband and able to frustrate he by stratagem
as the year go on he oppose she less and less whence rosamond conclude that he have learn the value of her opinion on the other hand she have a more thorough conviction of his talent now that he gain a good income and instead of the threaten cage in bride street provide one all flower and gilding fit for the bird of paradise that she resemble
in brief lydgate be what be call a successful man
but he die prematurely of diphtheria and rosamond afterwards marry an elderly and wealthy physician who take kindly to her four child
she make a very pretty show with her daughter drive out in her carriage and often speak of her happiness as a reward she do not say for what but probably she mean that it be a reward for her patience with tertius whose temper never become faultless and to the last occasionally let slip a bitter speech which be more memorable than the sign he make of his repentance
he once call she his basil plant and when she ask for an explanation say that basil be a plant which have flourish wonderfully on a murder man s brain
rosamond have a placid but strong answer to such speech
why then have he choose she
it be a pity he have not have mrs
ladislaw whom he be always praise and place above she
and thus the conversation end with the advantage on rosamond s side
but it would be unjust not to tell that she never utter a word in depreciation of dorothea keep in religious remembrance the generosity which have come to her aid in the sharp crisis of her life
dorothea herself have no dream of be praise above other woman feel that there be always something well which she might have do if she have only be well and know well
still she never repent that she have give up position and fortune to marry will ladislaw and he would have hold it the great shame as well as sorrow to he if she have repent
they be bind to each other by a love strong than any impulse which could have mar it
no life would have be possible to dorothea which be not fill with emotion and she have now a life fill also with a beneficent activity which she have not the doubtful pain of discover and mark out for herself
will become an ardent public man work well in those time when reform be begin with a young hopefulness of immediate good which have be much check in our day and get at last return to parliament by a constituency who pay his expense
dorothea could have like nothing well since wrong exist than that her husband should be in the thick of a struggle against they and that she should give he wifely help
many who know she think it a pity that so substantive and rare a creature should have be absorb into the life of another and be only know in a certain circle as a wife and mother
but no one state exactly what else that be in her power she ought rather to have do--not even sir james chettam who go no far than the negative prescription that she ought not to have marry will ladislaw
but this opinion of his do not cause a lasting alienation and the way in which the family be make whole again be characteristic of all concern
mr
brooke could not resist the pleasure of correspond with will and dorothea and one morning when his pen have be remarkably fluent on the prospect of municipal reform it run off into an invitation to the grange which once write could not be do away with at less cost than the sacrifice hardly to be conceive of the whole valuable letter
during the month of this correspondence mr
brooke have continually in his talk with sir james chettam be presuppose or hint that the intention of cut off the entail be still maintain and the day on which his pen give the daring invitation he go to freshitt expressly to intimate that he have a strong sense than ever of the reason for take that energetic step as a precaution against any mixture of low blood in the heir of the brookes
but that morning something exciting have happen at the hall
a letter have come to celia which make her cry silently as she read it and when sir james unused to see she in tear ask anxiously what be the matter she burst out in a wail such as he have never hear from she before
dorothea have a little boy
and you will not let i go and see she
and i be sure she want to see i
and she will not know what to do with the baby--she will do wrong thing with it
and they think she would die
it be very dreadful
suppose it have be i and little arthur and dodo have be hinder from come to see i
i wish you would be less unkind james good heaven celia say sir james much work upon what do you wish
i will do anything you like
i will take you to town to-morrow if you wish it and celia do wish it
it be after this that mr
brooke come and meet the baronet in the ground begin to chat with he in ignorance of the news which sir james for some reason do not care to tell he immediately
but when the entail be touch on in the usual way he say my dear sir it be not for i to dictate to you but for my part i would let that alone
i would let thing remain as they be mr
brooke feel so much surprised that he do not at once find out how much he be relieve by the sense that he be not expect to do anything in particular
such be the bent of celia s heart it be inevitable that sir james should consent to a reconciliation with dorothea and her husband
where woman love each other man learn to smother their mutual dislike
sir james never like ladislaw and will always prefer to have sir james s company mix with another kind they be on a footing of reciprocal tolerance which be make quite easy only when dorothea and celia be present
it become an understand thing that mr
and mrs
ladislaw should pay at least two visit during the year to the grange and there come gradually a small row of cousin at freshitt who enjoy play with the two cousin visit tipton as much as if the blood of these cousin have be less dubiously mixed
mr
brooke live to a good old age and his estate be inherit by dorothea s son who might have represent middlemarch but decline think that his opinion have less chance of be stifle if he remain out of door
sir james never cease to regard dorothea s second marriage as a mistake and indeed this remain the tradition concern it in middlemarch where she be speak of to a young generation as a fine girl who marry a sickly clergyman old enough to be her father and in little more than a year after his death give up her estate to marry his cousin--young enough to have be his son with no property and not well-bear
those who have not see anything of dorothea usually observe that she could not have be a nice woman else she would not have marry either the one or the other
certainly those determine act of her life be not ideally beautiful
they be the mixed result of young and noble impulse struggle amidst the condition of an imperfect social state in which great feeling will often take the aspect of error and great faith the aspect of illusion
for there be no creature whose inward be be so strong that it be not greatly determine by what lie outside it
a new theresa will hardly have the opportunity of reform a conventual life any more than a new antigone will spend her heroic piety in dare all for the sake of a brother s burial the medium in which their ardent deed take shape be forever go
but we insignificant people with our daily word and act be prepare the life of many dorothea some of which may present a far sad sacrifice than that of the dorothea whose story we know
she finely touch spirit have still its fine issue though they be not widely visible
her full nature like that river of which cyrus break the strength spend itself in channel which have no great name on the earth
but the effect of she be on those around she be incalculably diffusive for the grow good of the world be partly dependent on unhistoric act and that thing be not so ill with you and i as they might have be be half owe to the number who live faithfully a hidden life and rest in unvisited tomb
